# betterbrowsermcp

> **Project focus (May 2026+):** multi-agent, multi-tab browser
> automation, exercised via **website portfolio monetization** —
> real Stripe dashboard config (webhooks, products, payment links,
> customers, publishable key) is the validation surface.

This is the **authoritative project description** for the
betterbrowsermcp project. Read this first. Other docs
(`README.md`, `reference/ARCHITECTURE.md`, individual source files)
are details.

---

## TL;DR

`@nbiish/betterbrowsermcp` is a v0.3.0 Node.js MCP server that drives a
real browser (Brave/Chrome) through an extension, with **multi-agent
routing** and **multi-tab per agent**. It's a drop-in upgrade of
`@browsermcp/mcp@0.1.3` with the bugs and rough edges fixed.

| Repo | What |
|---|---|
| `nbiish/betterbrowsermcp` | The MCP server (this repo) |
| `nbiish/betterbrowsermcp-extension` | Chrome MV3 extension (companion) |

```bash
# Install
npm install -g @nbiish/betterbrowsermcp@latest
# Or use the local build
node /path/to/betterbrowsermcp/dist/index.js

# Run with one env var per agent
BROWSER_MCP_AGENT_ID=hermes BROWSER_MCP_PORT=9109 mcp-server-browsermcp
```

---

## What this is

A drop-in upgrade of `@browsermcp/mcp@0.1.3` (the upstream
single-process, single-tab browser automation MCP server) with
two coordinated upgrades:

1. **Multi-agent routing** (v0.2.0): one process per agent, each
   on its own port. No more `killProcessOnPort` murder between
   agents. Fixed the recursion bug in `server.close()`. Made the
   repo self-contained (inlined the monorepo workspace deps).

2. **Multi-tab per agent** (v0.3.0): one agent can drive multiple
   browser tabs concurrently. Per-tab labels. Per-agent active
   tab. Five new management tools (`browser_list_tabs`,
   `browser_open_tab`, `browser_close_tab`, `browser_rename_tab`,
   `browser_set_active_tab`). Every existing `browser_*` tool
   now accepts an optional `tabId` parameter.

---

## Real-world driver: website portfolio monetization

The betterbrowsermcp work is exercised end-to-end via configuring
**Stripe for a portfolio site**. The workflow:

- User has multiple tabs open in Brave: Stripe dashboard, OpenAI
  console, GitHub, deployment targets
- User binds all of them to the `hermes` agent
- LLM (driven by the user) sets up Stripe webhooks, creates
  products, generates payment links, extracts publishable keys
  for the local dev env — all by driving the actual browser
  with the user's real session
- LLM uses `browser_set_active_tab` to switch focus, then runs
  the standard `browser_click` / `browser_type` / `browser_snapshot`
  cycle on whichever tab needs work

**Why real browser?** Stripe's passkey auth (WebAuthn) doesn't
work with headless-style tools. betterbrowsermcp uses the user's
actual browser, so the user's real passkey credentials work. The
upstream had this capability but the multi-agent / port-fight bugs
made it unreliable for a multi-agent setup (which is required to
drive multiple sites in parallel).

---

## Architecture at a glance

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ LLM (Hermes, etc.)                                                │
│   ↕ stdio (MCP/JSON-RPC)                                          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ betterbrowsermcp (Node.js, ESM)                                  │
│   - Exposes browser_* tools (navigate, click, snapshot, ...)     │
│   - Exposes browser_*_tab management tools                       │
│   - Holds a single WebSocket per process (one process per agent) │
│   ↕ WebSocket (one per agent on a unique port)                   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Better Browser MCP Chrome extension (MV3)                        │
│   - Pool of WS connections (one per configured agent)            │
│   - Multi-tab routing: each tab has an agent binding + label     │
│   - Content script in every page: ARIA snapshots, clicks, etc.  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

For the full architecture (with line-by-line comparison to the
upstream we're forking), see **`reference/ARCHITECTURE.md`** —
this is the expert reference for how the system fits together.

For the vendored upstream source (frozen at v0.1.3), see
**`reference/upstream/`** — useful for diffing changes or
understanding the original design.

---

## Current state (v0.3.0)

| Repo | main | develop | npm |
|---|---|---|---|
| `nbiish/betterbrowsermcp` | v0.3.0 ✓ | v0.3.0 ✓ | `@nbiish/betterbrowsermcp@0.3.0` |
| `nbiish/betterbrowsermcp-extension` | v0.3.0 ✓ | v0.3.0 ✓ | _not published yet_ |

Both repos are on main at v0.3.0 with the full multi-tab surface
and the documentation set.

---

## How to use

### Build & run (one process per agent)

```bash
# Local build
git clone https://github.com/nbiish/betterbrowsermcp.git
cd betterbrowsermcp
npm install
npm run build
BROWSER_MCP_AGENT_ID=hermes BROWSER_MCP_PORT=9109 node dist/index.js

# Or after npm publish
npm install -g @nbiish/betterbrowsermcp@latest
BROWSER_MCP_AGENT_ID=hermes BROWSER_MCP_PORT=9109 mcp-server-browsermcp
```

### Configure in Hermes

In `~/.hermes/config.yaml`:

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  betterbrowsermcp:
    command: node
    args:
    - /path/to/betterbrowsermcp/dist/index.js
    env:
      BROWSER_MCP_AGENT_ID: hermes
      BROWSER_MCP_PORT: '9109'
```

Then add `betterbrowsermcp` to `platform_toolsets.cli` (and
`platform_toolsets.telegram` if you also use that platform). The
MCP tools will be exposed under the `mcp_betterbrowsermcp_*`
prefix once `/reload-mcp` runs (or on the next fresh session).

> **Hermes quirk (gotcha):** MCP server tools are REGISTERED with
> the registry at startup, but NOT EXPOSED to the LLM unless the
> server name is in `platform_toolsets.<your-platform>`. Default
> `cli` includes `browser`, `clarify`, `code_execution`, etc.
> but NOT your custom MCP servers. Add the new server name and
> `/reload-mcp` (or start a fresh session).

### Connect the browser

1. Open `brave://extensions` (or `chrome://extensions`)
2. Developer mode ON
3. Load unpacked from `nbiish/betterbrowsermcp-extension/`
4. In the popup, add an agent:
   - Agent ID: `hermes`
   - Port: `9109`
   - Auth token: (empty)
5. Open a tab to whatever site (e.g. `https://dashboard.stripe.com`)
6. Click the extension icon → "Bind to hermes"
7. Now the LLM can drive that tab

### Multi-tab workflow (the good stuff)

```
LLM: "What's bound?"
→ browser_list_tabs
← [tabId=12345 "Stripe dashboard", tabId=67890 "OpenAI console" ← ACTIVE]

LLM: "Switch to Stripe."
→ browser_set_active_tab(tabId=12345)

LLM: "Snapshot it."
→ browser_snapshot
← (full ARIA tree for the Stripe tab)

LLM: "Click Webhooks."
→ browser_click(element="Webhooks link in sidebar", ref="e14")
← ok + new snapshot

LLM: "Now go to OpenAI."
→ browser_set_active_tab(tabId=67890)
→ browser_snapshot
→ browser_click(element="API keys", ref="e7")
```

The LLM never specifies `tabId` for routine work — it just
manages active tab focus and calls the standard tools.

---

## Tool reference (v0.3.0)

### Standard browser tools (with optional `tabId`)

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `browser_navigate` | Navigate to a URL |
| `browser_click` | Click an element by snapshot ref |
| `browser_type` | Type text into an editable element |
| `browser_hover` | Hover over an element |
| `browser_select_option` | Select option(s) in a `<select>` |
| `browser_press_key` | Press a keyboard key |
| `browser_snapshot` | Capture ARIA tree with refs |
| `browser_screenshot` | Capture visible tab as PNG |
| `browser_get_console_logs` | Get page console logs |
| `browser_go_back` / `browser_go_forward` | History navigation |
| `browser_wait` | Sleep N seconds |
| `browser_drag` | Drag from one ref to another |

### Multi-tab management tools (v0.3.0+)

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `browser_list_tabs` | List bound tabs with labels + active marker |
| `browser_open_tab` | Open a new tab and bind it |
| `browser_close_tab` | Close a bound tab |
| `browser_rename_tab` | Set a human-readable label |
| `browser_set_active_tab` | Switch the active tab for unspecific calls |

### Response format

Every `browser_*` tool result includes `(tab: <label> [<tabId>])`
where applicable, so the LLM always knows which tab the call
landed on. ARIA snapshots embed a "Bound tabs for this agent"
block listing every available tab.

---

## Environment variables

| Var | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `BROWSER_MCP_AGENT_ID` | `default` | Agent identifier. Used in WS path (`/ws/<id>`) |
| `BROWSER_MCP_PORT` | `9009` | WebSocket port. Different ports for different agents |
| `BROWSER_MCP_BIND` | `127.0.0.1` | Bind address. Never set to `0.0.0.0` — exposes browser automation to network |
| `BROWSER_MCP_AUTH_TOKEN` | _(unset)_ | Optional shared secret for WS handshake |
| `BROWSER_MCP_WS_PATH_PREFIX` | `/ws` | WS path prefix. Final endpoint: `ws://host:port/ws/<agentId>` |
| `BROWSER_MCP_AGENT_SEPARATOR` | `__` | Tool name separator (e.g. `hermes__browser_navigate`) |
| `BROWSER_MCP_NO_KILL_PORT` | `true` | Refuse to silently kill other processes on port collision |

---

## Source layout

```
betterbrowsermcp/
├── README.md                   full setup, multi-agent + multi-tab docs
├── llms.txt                    this file — authoritative project description
├── package.json                v0.3.0, ESM, deps for standalone build
├── tsconfig.json               paths alias: @/* → src/*
├── src/
│   ├── config.ts               env var resolution, WS URL helpers
│   ├── context.ts              per-process Context (one WS = one tab)
│   ├── messaging.ts            WS protocol (request/response correlation)
│   ├── types.ts                Zod tool schemas (browser_* + manage_*)
│   ├── server.ts               MCP server, tool dispatch
│   ├── ws.ts                   WebSocket server with auth handshake
│   ├── index.ts                entry point + multi-process banner
│   ├── tools/
│   │   ├── common.ts           navigate, goBack, goForward, pressKey, wait
│   │   ├── snapshot.ts         snapshot, click, hover, type, selectOption
│   │   ├── custom.ts           screenshot, getConsoleLogs
│   │   ├── manage.ts           list_tabs, open_tab, close_tab, rename_tab, set_active_tab
│   │   └── tool.ts             Tool / ToolSchema / ToolResult types
│   ├── resources/
│   │   └── resource.ts         Resource types
│   └── utils.ts                wait() + non-murderous isPortInUse()
├── reference/                  expert reference material
│   ├── ARCHITECTURE.md         full upstream-vs-betterbrowsermcp comparison
│   ├── upstream/               vendored @browsermcp/mcp@0.1.3 (frozen)
│   │   ├── VENDORED.md         provenance + why vendored
│   │   ├── package.json        upstream manifest
│   │   ├── src/                upstream source (frozen, for diffing)
│   │   └── LICENSE             upstream Apache-2.0
│   └── diagrams/               (ascii art for the protocol flows)
└── .gitignore                  node_modules/, dist/, .agents/
```

---

## Critical workflow notes (gotchas hit during v0.3.0 dev)

1. **Never work on `main`.** All development on `develop` (or
   feature branches off `develop`). Promote develop → main only
   after validation.

2. **Per-process port.** One MCP process per agent, each on its
   own port. `killProcessOnPort` is gone — port collisions are
   hard errors now. This was the root cause of the upstream's
   keepalive-every-90s pain between agents.

3. **`/reload-mcp` after config changes.** When you update
   `~/.hermes/config.yaml` (add a new MCP server, change env vars,
   update platform_toolsets), the running gateway needs a reload
   to pick up the changes. `/reload-mcp` in the TUI input box
   (NOT the terminal), or restart the gateway for fresh sessions.

4. **Hermes's built-in `browser` toolset (Camofox-backed) takes
   precedence over MCP tools with colliding names.** If you see
   `browser_navigate` in the available list but not
   `mcp_betterbrowsermcp_browser_navigate`, the built-in is
   winning the name collision. Fix: add `betterbrowsermcp` to
   `platform_toolsets.cli`. We discovered this during the v0.3.0
   release.

5. **`/reload-mcp` re-spawns MCP children but does NOT re-inject
   the LLM's system prompt.** A toolset config change only takes
   effect for FRESH conversations. If the new tools don't appear
   in the active LLM, start a new conversation.

6. **Orphan v0.3.0 servers on port 9109 will block `/reload-mcp`.**
   If you ran `node dist/index.js` for testing and forgot to
   kill it, the Hermes-spawned child fails to bind. The
   mcp-stderr.log shows "Port 9109 is already in use". Fix:
   `kill <orphan-pid>` (NOT `pkill` — Hermes blocks `pkill`).

7. **Stripe passkey auth requires the real browser session.**
   The extension drives the user's actual Chrome — the user's
   passkey credentials work. Headless-style tools that bypass
   the browser session won't satisfy Stripe's WebAuthn.

8. **Content scripts don't re-inject on already-open tabs.** When
   you update the extension's content.js, the running tab still
   has the old content script. Refresh the page (Cmd+R or
   `Cmd+Shift+R` for hard reload) to pick up the new version.

9. **The MCP server's snapshot/press_key/select_option/get_console_logs
   were silently broken in v0.3.0 because of a case-name mismatch
   in the content script.** Fixed in v0.3.0 patch — content
   script now normalizes via `stripBrowserPrefix(msg.type)`. The
   symptom was: snapshot returned `undefined` and tools silently
   fell through to the default error branch. The fix is on
   main at commit `b5ff9c2`.

10. **Screenshot needs `host_permissions: ["<all_urls>"]`.** The
    default was localhost-only, which broke `captureVisibleTab`
    on HTTPS sites like Stripe. Fixed in the same commit.

---

## Why this exists (the war story)

The upstream `@browsermcp/mcp@0.1.3` is great for one LLM driving
one browser tab. It's broken for:

- **Multiple agents** (Hermes, OMP, Codex) — each tries to bind
  port 9009, the `killProcessOnPort` murders the others in a
  90-second keepalive cycle
- **Multiple tabs per agent** — there's no concept of "this
  Stripe tab" vs "this OpenAI tab" for the same agent
- **Stripe passkey auth** — must drive real Chrome, not a
  virtual browser

betterbrowsermcp is the v0.3.0+ answer to all three. See
`reference/ARCHITECTURE.md` for the full before/after and
`README.md` for user-facing docs.

---

## Related repos

- `nbiish/betterbrowsermcp-extension` — the Chrome MV3 extension
- `BrowserMCP/mcp` (upstream) — `https://github.com/BrowserMCP/mcp`
- `BrowserMCP/extension` (upstream ext) — `https://github.com/BrowserMCP/extension`
